data.title: Artesian Resources: Attractively Valued Among Its Peers
data.description:
Artesian Resources Corporation (NASDAQ:ARTNA) is a water utility operating primarily in Delaware but also in Maryland and Pennsylvania. Artesian has been in the water business since 1905. In the past decade, its revenue has increased a little less than 50%, while the net income has nearly doubled. In addition, Artesian offers an attractive valuation and dividend yield, a clear path to growth, and a solid balance sheet.

Geographically focused water utility

Artesian Resources is one of the smallest listed water utility companies in the U.S. Artesian’s market capitalization is only $340 million compared to the billions of some of its peers. Its water and wastewater systems serve over 300,000 people, primarily in Delaware. Artesian generates most of its revenue in water sales, while wastewater contributes less than 15% to sales. Artesian reported Q4 results on the 12th of March. Its annual revenue grew 4.6%, and net income increased 11.9%. Its earnings per share increased from $1.98 to $2.21.

A completion of a key project drives future growth

In 2025 Artesian invested nearly $32 million (net, after contributions in aid of construction) in the water infrastructure. This year the company expects to invest another $64 million. That is nearly one-fifth of its market capitalization. The significant investments should carry earnings growth for several years to come.

The key ongoing project is the so-called SRRF wastewater treatment plant. Artesian inherited the project by acquiring Tidewater Environmental Services in 2022 from Middlesex Water Company. The second phase of the project is nearing its completion, and the third phase has received an approval, which will double the capacity compared to phase two. Last year its wastewater revenue grew approximately 11%. The further growth of wastewater sales will add to a more stable revenue base.

Artesian’s water sales revenue increased 3.2% thanks to two temporary rate increases in 2025. Artesian has applied for a 12.4% base rate increase, while in 2024, Artesian obtained a 15.2% revenue increase. Since the majority of its revenue comes from Delaware, Artesian’s rate base growth is at the mercy of the Delaware Public Service Commission. Lack of geographical diversification is one of Artesian’s downsides.

Artesian could be an attractive acquisition target for American Water, which derives one-fourth of its revenues from Pennsylvania. However, Artesian is controlled by the Taylor founding family by class B shares and holding two out of five board seats. Class A shares (ARTNA) have no voting power. Geographical concentration and dual share class structure are likely reasons why Artesian is valued as it is.

Water utilities and their share prices are sensitive to changes in interest rates. Among the small listed water utilities, Artesian is one of the least indebted. It carries $174 million of total debt, which is almost entirely fixed rate. In 2025, Artesian paid $8.7 million in interest against operating income of $35.6 million. While Artesian should not be challenged by its debt, increasing debt and rising inflation and interest rates could pressure Artesian’s financial performance and stock price.

Valuation is attractive in relation to future growth and peers

Due to steadily growing earnings, a flat or declining share price, and only occasional share issuance, Artesian is trading at a historically low valuation. Its earnings per share have grown at a 5.8% CAGR for the past ten years. Historically seen, Artesian is currently fairly valued. If Artesian’s valuation were to be heading towards its five-year average P/E multiple of 22, an upside would be significant. In the higher rate environment, that appears unlikely.

Artesian’s significant investments should deliver earnings growth in the next two to three years. Assuming $96 million of investments, the current capital structure, 9% ROE, and a cost of debt of 5.5% should bring in approximately $0.5 uplift in the earnings per share. Assuming no change in the valuation, applying a P/E multiple of 15x to earnings of $2.7 per share would result in a target price of $40 in a couple of years, leaving out other growth levers from the equation.

Artesian is interestingly valued compared to its peers, other smaller water utilities such as Middlesex Water, The York Water, and Global Water Resources. It has only slightly lower profitability but trades at a P/E multiple 35% lower than MSEX and YORW. Regarding growth, Artesian has grown revenues slower but improved its profitability faster. The valuation difference is especially evident when looking at the EV/EBITDA multiple. Simply applying the five-year average EV/EBITDA multiple of 13.6x on Artesian would result in a target price of $47.

Dividend yield is attractive for an income investor

Artesian’s annual dividend payout is $1.25, translating to a dividend yield of 3.9%. The yield currently stands much higher than on average in the past, averaging 2.5% in the past five years. Artesian has increased the dividend for over three decades. For the past five years, the growth rate has been 4.2%. The payout ratio of 56% ensures there’s room for further increases. From a cash flow perspective, the dividend consumes $12.7 million in relation to the previously mentioned operating income of $35.6 million.

Conclusion

Artesian is an attractive choice among smaller water utilities. There is a rather clear path to further growth from an attractive level of valuation. The lag between the investments and revenue adds uncertainty to the timing of earnings growth. Meanwhile, an investor can enjoy one of the highest yields among the small water utilities.

data.title: Atlassian: The Stock Collapsed, But The Business Did Not (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment thesis

My thesis on investing in Atlassian (TEAM) has evolved since the last article I wrote. Unfortunately, I've never had an opportunity to modify my opinion. I went from "Strong Buy" to "Hold" primarily based on the fact that Atlassian is no longer a "trust your instincts" kind of stock where you can buy it simply because it appears cheap. While the decline in price has been extreme, upon further examination the business looks much more solid than the chart suggests. The revenue growth continues to be healthy, the advancement of their cloud continues to move forward rapidly, they were able to mitigate the contracted demand reasonably effectively, and they have developed a unique ecosystem of products around Jira, Confluence, and Jira Service Management which makes it increasingly difficult for competitors to create comparable products that fit into their customers' workflows. The most recent quarter was not poor by any stretch of the imagination, rather it illustrated a company that continues to execute.

That said, I don't believe this is the right context to aggressively increase a position. The issue now centers on whether Atlassian will be able to protect their per-seat economics, continue to enjoy pricing power, and prove that artificial intelligence (AI) will serve as a means of creating new sources of monetization and will not create additional downward pressures on multiples and growth quality. Those risks are no longer speculative. Indeed, it is exactly those same factors which would constitute a "bearish" or "negative" case for this particular stock (the increased competition, potentially fewer work stations as a result of improved productivity through AI, customer resistance to higher prices, and the operational risks that arise from recent corporate restructuring initiatives).

I continue to hold an amount of stock in Atlassian as part of my portfolio. I have a strong commitment to retaining an investment position with Atlassian due to quality products and deeply integrated software that is transitioning from on premise deployments to cloud-based delivery models. The operational resiliency exhibited by Atlassian demonstrates sufficient ability for a "wait" to be warranted. Sure, at these prices, it seems like a golden opportunity to buy shares, especially with a long-term perspective. However, I've personally decided not to increase my current position, firstly due to the current macroeconomic environment (the Federal Reserve's restrictive monetary policy, the ongoing conflict, etc.) and, secondly, because I need concrete evidence from management and financial data demonstrating that Atlassian's historically high valuation premium is justified again. If I didn't own shares, however, I might consider it.

What has really changed at Atlassian

The main difference now from a couple of months back is that the market no longer views Atlassian as a typical 'premium software' play which the market inherently trusts in advance. Now the market wants proof.

When we look at the last set of quarterly figures, I don't see an organization that is going downhill. Rather, I see a business that continues to be driven by cloud, enterprise, and AI, but is being seen as though it is no longer worth valuing like the previous premium valuation model. Of course the world is changing and AI is producing large splits in the technology space particularly within the software area. But when we take a closer view at the most recent quarterly figures, during Q2FY26, Atlassian reported $1.59 Billion in revenues, a 23% increase year-over-year; $1.07 Billion in Cloud Revenues, a 26% increase year-over-year, and Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO) of $3.81 Billion, a 44% increase year-over-year. Current RPO increased 30% to $2.7B. These figures are too strong to classify the business as broken.

As I mentioned before, the quarter went well. The company closed a record number of deals above $1M in ACV (nearly doubled year-over-year), surpassed 600 customers with over $1M in ARR (up almost 40%), and confirmed a cloud NRR above 120% for the third consecutive quarter. Furthermore, customers with over $10,000 in cloud ARR rose to 55,369, +12% year-over-year. I believe this is an intriguing sign because it indicates that Atlassian is not merely protecting its customer base, but that Atlassian is also increasing its depth and value with existing Enterprise Customers.

While the attention of the Market has moved from evaluating past performance metrics (e.g. "How many new customers can we acquire", "How much Cloud Revenue are we going to generate"), today, everyone wants to understand the quality of the future growth. And, therefore, the conversation is significantly different. We all understand that the market's perception has changed dramatically. Historically, it was enough to simply say "The cloud is exploding" and that was enough to create enthusiasm. Now, it must be proven and demonstrated whether or not AI will continue to enhance the value of the platform, while simultaneously supporting a user based pricing model (while maintaining pricing power), and/or slowing the monetization rate of the product over time. Management has provided some insight into their views/clarification, and indicated that the cloud revenue in Q2 was a direct result of expansion of paid locations, migration of users from on premise data center environments to cloud environments, cross selling opportunities, and an increase in Average Revenue Per User ("ARPU"). Furthermore, management has increased guidance for fiscal year 2026, projecting total revenue to increase 22.0% and cloud revenue to increase 24.3%.

However, even though Atlassian has provided some positive outlook indicators, Atlassian itself has acknowledged that the guidance provided still reflects a conservative/risk averse approach, due to continued macroeconomic uncertainties and the evolving nature of the enterprise software marketplace.

Valuation: Cheap Enough to Hold, Not Clear Enough to Buy More

I feel that the main concern regarding Atlassian today is not if the stock is "cheap" on an absolute basis. Honestly, that's what it has become. My biggest concern is whether the devaluation (i.e., the fact that the stock has fallen) will be sufficient to support a return to growth in the price.

Currently, TEAM trades at roughly 12.7x next year's earnings, versus about 71.7x for peers and around 48.1x for its own two-year average.

Additionally, the enterprise value/sales ratio has experienced a substantial rerating, approximately 3.0 times trailing sales, which is significantly lower than both the company's own historical performance and many other high-quality companies within the software space. Furthermore, Atlassian shares are trading at an approximate 82% discount to their peer group averages; however, historically, Atlassian shares have traded at a premium of 19% to their peer groups over the last 2-years. That is simply too extreme of a number and I would assert that fairly.

While an extremely low multiple can certainly provide some evidence to support a "buy" thesis, it is not enough by itself to create a reason to go long. I do not believe the markets are merely forcing down Atlassian shares due to poor sentiment in software. I believe there is another factor involved. I believe the market is attempting to determine if future growth still warrants a premium. While last quarter's results were excellent (revenue of $1.59B increased by 23% from prior year; cloud revenue of $1.07B increased by 26%; RPO of $3.81B increased by 44%; current RPO of $2.7B increased by 30%), management raised their FY26 forecasted revenue growth rate to 22.0% and their cloud revenue forecasted growth rate to 24.3%. Therefore, while I am not seeing a breakage in the business model (the business continues to execute), I am seeing that the markets aren't providing the same level of premiums they provided previously.

I am opposed to aggressive buying right now but likewise, I feel it does not make sense for me to sell today. I'm not selling because at these prices, the market is already factoring in a very severe scenario, almost like a company entering a phase of structural decline, while official data continues to show double-digit growth. Personally, selling here would mean treating Atlassian as if the thesis were broken. And reading the numbers, I don't see that.

That being said, I will not buy more shares of TEAM. This is not a decision I made lightly. In my view, there are many factors beyond management control such as the timing of when customers migrate their data centers to the cloud. Further, although the benefits of Data Center EOL will provide some additional margin in FY26, the shareholder letter makes it clear that once those benefits start to wane - Data Center growth is expected to slow down dramatically in FY27 - both due to base effect and increased cloud migration along with less robust seat expansion. Therefore, while I do feel that the valuation of TEAM is much more favorable today compared to just a couple of months back - I believe we need more evidence before returning to increasing our exposure in an affirmative manner.

Risk and Why I Will Sell or Add

I will only sell this stock when a material weakness in the business (structural) emerges. I consider the following potential weaknesses as "material":

  • A significant decline in the rate of growth in the core business.
  • A decline in NRR for Cloud-based products.
  • Weaknesses in expanding seats across many consecutive periods.
  • Signs that price elasticity and cross-sell opportunities are diminishing.

If the company will experience a loss of customer penetration, then my long thesis would likely begin to erode. Conversely, I would only increase my ownership in TEAM if there were evidence of continued success operationally (not just due to low multiples). Therefore, I would only buy more shares of TEAM if there was:

  • Clear evidence of continued solid cloud based growth.
  • Strong execution from the Enterprise division.
  • Credible monetization of Artificial Intelligence capabilities.

Conclusion

Atlassian is no longer a stock I am going to purchase solely based upon "narrative". I believe that chapter is closed. As such, I believe the investors has been more demanding; I believe that is a good thing. While a lower valuation (multiple) is nothing more than a starting point, it is not an investment thesis.

I will not sell because I do not believe the business is broken. I believe the product offerings continue to be integrated within the customer base. I also believe the cloud-based growth continues to remain strong and I see that the enterprises continue to find value with their utilization of the platform. And, I believe contracted demand continues to be strong. Based on what I can gather from reviewing the operational data, I do not thing the Company is in decline.

I am looking for more than just a cheap stock. On the opposite end, I will be interested in buying additional shares if the management team proves to me that their vision for the companies' future will continue to justify paying a premium. That means I want to see long term momentum in the cloud and continued successful execution of enterprise strategies. Most importantly, I want to see a strong monetization of AI capabilities that add value to the Atlassian platform instead of decreasing its value. Once I have seen these types of positive developments, I believe purchasing additional shares of TEAM is a reasonable course of action.

In this moment, I prefer to maintain a Hold position in TEAM because the fundamentals appear stronger than the stock. If you are an investor who does not own TEAM shares, you may now be at a reasonable juncture to begin acquiring shares incrementally. If you already own TEAM shares, I believe your competitive advantage lies not in courage but in self-discipline.

data.title: Alvopetro: High Dividend Yield, Benefiting From Higher Oil Prices (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

As it has been a few years since I looked at Alvopetro Energy Ltd (ALVOF) (ALV:CA), I thought the current strong Brent oil price environment warranted another look at Alvopetro. I did well on the stock a few years ago, as the market didn’t fully realize the company’s realized natural gas price is partially based on the Brent oil price as well. And when the oil price went down and production volumes were going to be disappointing, I exited my position right on time, as the share price lost about half of its value). With Brent at or above $100/barrel these days, I expect to see a strong first semester from Alvopetro.

The website does not always contain unique URLs, but you can find all relevant press releases here.

A robust result in Q4 2025, with more to come in Q1 2026

In the final quarter of 2025, the company produced a total of just over 2,700 barrels of oil equivalent per day. Perhaps it’s a bit strange to use a boe as a standard considering the very vast majority of the production consists of natural gas.

As the table below indicates, the company received a very strong C$9.97 per Mcf of natural gas, which is, of course, the main reason for its high margins and why the lack of economies of scale (being a small producer) doesn’t hurt Alvopetro too much.

Thanks to a very robust production performance in the final quarter of the year, the weighted average output increased from less than 1,800 boe/day in 2024 to just over 2,500 boe/day in 2025. And with an average realized natural gas price exceeding C$10/Mcf during 2025, Alvopetro’s revenue and cash flows were very robust.

The company reported a total revenue of just under $58 million and a net revenue of just under $56 million after deducting the royalties and accounting for $2 million in “other income” (which is related to interest income and tax recoveries).

As you can see in the income statement above, the operating expenses remained extremely low, and this resulted in a pre-tax income of $27 million, indicating the margin on the incremental revenue was very strong. After deducting the relevant corporate taxes, Alvopetro reported a net income of $23.1 million for an EPS of US$0.62.

That’s excellent, but I was also interested in the cash flow result, as Alvopetro’s cash flows usually are even stronger than the net income.

In FY 2025, Alvopetro reported an operating cash flow of $40.6 million, and even after deducting the $2.3 million in lease payments, the adjusted operating cash flow was still just over $38 million. This was more than sufficient to cover the $3.4 million in capex, while the $30.1 million in property, plant and equipment capex was also fully covered. And while the combination of capex + dividends was not entirely covered, there is a good reason for this, as Alvopetro invested heavily in its operations, and the majority of the capex spent in 2025 was related to growth. For 2026, for instance, Alvopetro is suggesting a capex of $21 million in Brazil (mainly focusing on the expansion of infrastructure ahead of boosting the production rates to ‘fill’ the expanded infrastructure assets) and $1.5 million on its Canadian assets.

The longer-term potential of Alvopetro

The lower capex in 2026 will very likely go hand in hand with a much higher operating cash flow. Not only because the Brent oil price is substantially higher right now, but also because production will be higher.

Keep in mind the $38 million adjusted operating cash flow in FY 2025 was based on an average production rate of 2,500 boe/day, but as some production only came online in H2 2025, the average daily output in 2026 should be much stronger. The company recently disclosed production rates north of 3,000 boe/day in February, and this paves the way for an adjusted operating cash flow of around $11 million in Q1 2026, increasing towards $13-$15 million in Q2 when the higher Brent oil price starts to play a role. And that cash flow will be a tremendous help to fund the multi-year growth plan.

And this is all underpinned by a robust reserve calculation. At the end of 2025, the company had a net attributable 12.2 million barrels of oil equivalent in its 2P reserves, resulting in a net after-tax NPV10 of $312 million.

Even if you want to be more conservative and use a 10% discount rate for the developed wells, a 15% discount on the undeveloped wells, and 20% on the probable reserves, the after-tax NPV10% would still come in at around $245 million. And considering this is based on a Brent oil price of just $63/barrel for this year and $70/barrel in 2027, the after-tax NPV is likely substantially higher now, given the higher-than-anticipated cash flows on the back of the Brent oil price.

Investment thesis

Alvopetro’s share price has been strong recently, but I don’t think the market fully realizes the potential here. Q1 will be strong on the back of very robust production volumes, but the much stronger Brent oil price will only be incorporated from May 1, when the quarterly pricing is established. I think the market could be pleasantly surprised by the H1 2025 results, which will bolster the net cash position (currently at approximately $12 million). I expect Alvopetro to generate at least $25 million in net free cash flow this year, and this includes an anticipated oil price decrease to $70 Brent in the second half of the year.

Despite Alvopetro’s robust share price performance, the company is still attractive as it remains on the verge of a substantial production and margin increase. Meanwhile, the recently increased dividend ($0.12 per quarter per share) represents an attractive 7% yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pebblebrook Hotel Trust: High Yield Vs. High Risk In A Cyclical Sector
data.description:
The hotel REITs remain оne of the most cyclical and economically sensitive groups. Pebblebrook Hotel Trust (PEB) stands out with its high yield on preferred stocks, but also with a higher risk profile that requires careful analysis. In the following lines, we look at the company's key financial indicators, capital structure, and the yield of its financial instruments.

PEB overview

At the end of 2025, PEB had close to $5.34 billion in total assets and around $2.5 billion in total debt. The total equity is around $2.56 billion, and the preferred equity is ~$754 million. The EBITDA is around $302.9 million. PEB's current market capitalization is approximately $1.44 billion. The company owns 44 hotels in 13 markets, with a focus on urban and resort segments.

We calculate the asset yield of PEB ((total revenue - operating expenses + depreciation and amortization) / total assets) of around 5.66%, and the asset coverage ratio (total assets/debt) close to 214%. The operating expenses as a percentage of the total revenue, excluding depreciation and amortization, are around 79.5%. The interest expenses as a percentage of the total debt are close to 4.13%. We use the price-to-book ratio, which is currently close to 0.58, to calculate the market-adjusted ratios. This means the market is valuing the company's assets at a discount of over 40%. This alone is not a buy signal, but combined with 96% unencumbered assets, it shows that even in the event of a forced sale, creditors have a significant buffer. We subtract total equity from the total assets and add the new one by multiplying by the price-to-book. The market-adjusted asset yield (total revenue- operating expenses+depreciation and amortization/(total assets-total equity + (total equity*P/B)) is close to 7.09%, and the market-adjusted asset coverage ratio is around 170.7% (debt/ (total assets-total equity + (total equity*P/B)). The market-adjusted asset yield versus the cost of debt of 4.13% yields a spread of about 3%—this is the operating engine that supports preferred stock payments. A narrowing of this spread is a key risk to watch.

The forward price to AFFO is close to 12.64 - the expected AFFO yield is 7.91%.

Below are shown the basic valuation metrics:

Preferred Stocks

Currently, all PEB's preferred stocks are traded below par. (PEB.PR.E) and (PEB.PR.F) are callable yet, (PEB.PR.H) has close to 0.34 years to call with a call date on 07/27/2026, and (PEB.PR.G) has close to 0.13 years to call with a call date on 05/13/2026.

PEB.PR.E was issued on 02/27/2013 with a coupon rate of 6.38% and a spread to the treasury yield of around 3.28%. Currently, the stock has a yield close to 8%, and this spread is around 3.11%. In the next chart, you can see its potential yields versus prices.

PEB.PR.H's issue date was on 7/22/2021, coupon rate 5.70%, and spread to the treasury yield 3.8%. The yield to worst of the stock, at the time of writing, is around 7.99%, and the spread to the treasury yield is close to 3.09%.

PEB.PR.G has a yield to worst of close to 7.97%. The stock was issued on 05/06/2021 with a coupon rate of 6.38% and a spread to the treasury yield of around 4.08%. This spread is currently close to 3.07%.

PEB.PR.F was issued on 05/18/2016 with a coupon rate of 6.3% and a spread to the treasury yield of around 3.65%. Currently, the stock has a yield close to 7.79%, and this spread is around 2.89%.

OTC Bonds

Below is shown PEB's over-the-counter bond, with a close to 5.8% yield to maturity and a "B2" credit rating by Moody's. The credit spread is close to 1.9% currently. If we assume that preferred stocks are 2 notches down in credit rating, and add +0.25% for each notch for the OTC bond, we will get nearly a 0.7% risk premium spread between the two.

Credit Metrics

Moody’s gives a B2 rating to the OTC bond, which reflects the higher credit risk, placing it in the "Junk" ratings. However, we want to look at the specific credit metrics:

- Gross assets ~ $7 billion.

- Unencumbered assets — close to 96%. This is a relatively strong indicator reflecting high financial flexibility in the event of a possible refinancing.

- Secured debt ratio — only about 3%, a low level, and further supports flexibility.

- Total debt + preferred stock as a percentage of gross assets is approximately 42%. Net debt to EBITDA is about 9.8x. This is high leverage and one of the main risks; even a moderate decline in revenues can put pressure on interest coverage.

- EBITDA covers interest payments and dividends on preferred shares nearly 2 times (relatively thin coverage for a cyclical business).

Key Risks

1. Cyclicality of the sector

The hotel segment is highly dependent on the economic cycle. In a recession, revenues can drop sharply while fixed costs remain — this directly pressures EBITDA and the company's ability to service its debt.

2. High leverage

- Net Debt / EBITDA close to 10x is a key risk. If operating results deteriorate by 15-20%, interest coverage would fall below the critical threshold of 1.5x.

3. High operating expenses

- over 80% operating expenses.

4. Refinancing risk

At higher intеrеst rates, PEB will have tо refinance its debt at a higher cost, and from that, interest expenses increase. This reduces prоfits and the frее cash with which it cоvers the interest and the dividеnds. As a result, the risk fоr investоrs increases, especially in a dоwnturn in the economy.

Conclusion

PEB's preferred stocks offer an attractive yield of around 8%, supported by a relatively good asset base and a high level оf unencumbered assets. Hоwever, the high leverage and cyclical nature оf the hotel business significantly increase the risk, especially in an economic dоwnturn.

data.title: Meituan: A 'Buy' On Narrowing Losses And Regulatory Tailwinds (Upgrade)
data.description:
I've turned bullish on Meituan (MPNGF) (MPNGY).

MPNGF's losses are getting smaller, as seen with its 4th quarter results. Its financial outlook is boosted by favorable regulatory developments.

Its 1Q24 outperformance and potential Q2 revenue deceleration were outlined in my previous June 11, 2024, update.

My Bet Is On Further Bottom-Line Improvement

MPNGF's latest financials were released in an announcement issued on Thursday, March 26.

The firm recorded negative earnings of CNY15,080 million for Oct-Dec '25. That's better than the preceding quarter's CNY16.0 billion deficit.

I've identified its "Core Local Commerce/CLC" or "Food Delivery/FD" unit as the difference-maker.

The FD-related loss shrank from 3Q2025's CNY14,071 million to 4Q2025's CNY10,046 million. MPNGF noted at the results meeting that it "pulled back resources from those low Average Order Value (AOV)" transactions. My calculations suggest that the loss-per-order metric was reduced from CNY2.3 to CNY1.9 during the same period.

It's also encouraging to know the group has crossed into the black for a new market. MPNGF's briefing remarks indicated that "Keeta (FD sub-brand) launched in Hong Kong/HK in May '23, and has broken even in October '25." This took about two and a half years.

Looking ahead, I believe the loss contraction trend can be sustained.

As per S&P Capital IQ, the sell-side is projecting a bottom line of -CNY6.7B for the first three months of '26. This is consistent with guidance. CLC's Q1 "losses (are) projected to halve sequentially" based on the management's communications with analysts.

MPNGF emphasized the importance of "promoting the healthy and sustainable development of the industry" in the Q4 disclosure. It also drew attention to the lower "user incentives as we focused on quality growth." I infer from these statements that the enterprise will continue to prioritize high-AOV bookings.

The profitability of overseas operations is on an uptrend too. At the earnings call, the CEO shared his expectations of Keeta achieving "its first profitable month in Saudi Arabia (KSA) much faster than in Hong Kong." This implies that its KSA business is likely to achieve breakeven in Feb. 2027 at the latest using HK's benchmark.

You should also be aware that MPNGF incurred significant start-up investments in certain Middle East nations during 2H25. These expenses shouldn't recur in 1Q2026.

Chinese Regulators' Recent Moves Should Benefit The Company

Bloomberg reported on this Wednesday that "SAMR’s website reposted an opinion piece from the state-backed newspaper Economic Daily." That article "called for an end to price wars in the (food delivery) industry." SAMR refers to the PRC's "State Administration for Market Regulation."

The above-mentioned media publication revealed some key statistics. I'm shocked that MPNGF and competitors allocated a staggering $11-14 billion to FD subsidies last year. It also highlighted that food/dining accounted for almost one-third of China's "Consumer Price Index" or "CPI."

These numbers explain why the authorities are so concerned about the intense rivalry in the domestic FD space. CPI is a critical input that influences policymaking. Food pricing has been artificially depressed, which distorts this economic metric. Separately, it will be in the nation's interest for corporations to invest in technological advancements. On the flip side, you should agree that engaging in a zero-sum game to seize market share is value-destructive.

At the analyst briefing, MPNGF stressed that "a more regulated market can help shift competition from pure subsidy wars toward innovation." My take is that China's FD players should be compelled to cut back on user incentives.

Also, the regulators' anti-competitive stance is good news for Meituan's "in-store" division, which provides hospitality booking services.

"The SAMR has commenced an investigation" against Trip.com (TCOM) "pursuant to the Anti-Monopoly Law" in early-2026 according to SA News. This means that this leading "Online Travel Agent/OTA" will likely be prevented from signing hotel exclusivity deals going forward.

I take the view that this development will give MPNGF's "in-store" arm the opportunity to enlarge its room inventory. This segment's growth was previously constrained by supply limitations, which shouldn't be an issue anymore.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Variant Views

If the firm isn't sufficiently disciplined in managing order mix and discretionary spending, its future losses may widen. Investors tend to shun loss-making businesses that have limited turnaround potential.

The food delivery operators might not make a shift away from existing price-focused strategies. This assumes that the regulators aren't serious about investigating and penalizing errant parties.

My Conclusion

I've revised MPNGF's rating from "Hold" to "Buy."

This stock is currently trading at a forward "Enterprise Value-to-Sales" or "EV/S" of below 1 or 0.94 times.

The next 12 months' EV/S multiple for its listed comparable, DoorDash (DASH), is a far more demanding 3.6x.

DASH was profitable for the full year FY25. MPNGF is expected to be in the black again by 4Q26, according to S&P Capital IQ consensus. That could serve as a re-rating trigger to narrow the peer valuation gap.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Heritage Insurance Holdings: Valuation And Waning Technicals Warrant Caution
data.description:
Establishing property and casualty or P&C insurance business in disaster-prone areas can be strategic for secure demand and revenue streams. However, this comes with a great risk as hurricanes, floods, wildfires, and snowstorms can lead to massive claims and losses. With that, several key players are working on reducing its risk exposure to improve their profitability and sustainability. This is what I observe in the underwriting strategies of Heritage Insurance Holdings, Inc. (HRTG), another Florida-based P&C insurer. I understand its move considering the challenges it has faced in recent years. Fundamentals are more attractive and sound, making it well-positioned against a more volatile market environment. My only concern is that valuation appears fully priced, which may pose downside risks at this level. Technicals adhere to it as momentum starts to wane.

Heritage Insurance: How It Has Been Lately

The P&C insurance industry in Florida has experienced more peaceful and stable market conditions in the past two quarters. There were several hurricanes during this period. Yet, there were not as destructive and intense than those who have hit the Atlantic coastline in the past three years. That is why businesses like Heritage Insurance Holdings, Inc. benefited from more favorable trends as it incurred much lower claims and losses expenses. This also helped it stronger pricing while maintaining its disciplined underwriting policies.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $215.3M, up by 2.4% YoY from $210.3M. This was also its best performance since Q2 2025, which served as its comeback from its slowing growth in Q2 and Q3 2025. I think this is quite impressive considering the contraction of its operation capacity. In fact, its gross premiums earned still increased despite the notable reduction of its policies in force. If we divide gross premiums by the total policies, HRTG generated an average amount of $1,012 ($361.7M/357,275 policies) versus $925 YoY. This shows its stronger pricing power. Aside from improved market conditions, one may assume that its contraction was done to focus on stronger target markets and inflation-resilient clients. After all, it has exposure both in personal and commercial lines. Its investment management strategies also remained prudent and paid off, as shown by the notable increase in net investment income.

Moreover, its lower operating capacity allowed it to greatly reduce its total expenses. As a result, its operating margin exceeded 40%, which was nearly thrice as much as 15% YoY. Lastly, its net income margin rose to 31.0% versus 9.8% YoY.

Key Considerations

As a P&C insurer, floods and tropical storms remain a challenge for HRTG and peers. When tropical storms and hurricanes are intense, there may also be more claims and losses filed by policyholders. The consolation is that these products are a staple for households and businesses in Florida and neighboring states. So, demand in these area are sure. However, it must also consider competition since there are also large players there. Some examples include Universal Insurance (UVE), American Coastal Insurance (ACIC), and HCI Group (HCI).

Another factor to note is inflation because it remains stubborn, which may be exacerbated by new tariffs and skyrocketing oil prices. This may affect the purchasing power of policyholders. This may also affect the capacity of the existing policyholders to sustain their scheduled payments. Additionally, HRTG may have to face higher cost pressures, which may affect its pricing flexibility and margins.

Potential Mitigants

HRTG is not entirely safe from these headwinds, but I believe that its business nature itself can provide some insulation. Insurance products are not like a typical product or service that consumers can reduce or not buy when prices increase. After all, their prices remain the same within the repayment period regardless of inflation and interest rates. For existing policyholders, it is not that simple to surrender their policies, especially if they have already been paying for this for years. With that, its revenues are typically secure and stable. In addition, it has exposure to commercial lines. Business owners are typically more inflation-resilient, so they are more capable of paying for their policies.

Another core strength of it is its strategic underwriting policies. HRTG has a wide range of geographical diversification. It has a strong market presence on the East Coast, allowing it to maintain a strong brand recognition in the region. But what I really find interesting is its presence in Hawaii. But one should note its decreasing policies in force to reduce its exposure to underpriced personal lines, roofing scams, and higher-risk areas, allowing it to become more selective in its underwriting. This is why the average premiums earned per policy increased YoY. In short, it has fewer but better policies. And now, we have seen it reverse its growth in the past two years.

Its robust Balance Sheet is another aspect to note. Most of its assets are liquid. In fact, the combined amount of invests and cash comprise nearly 60% of the total. This is enough to cover 99% of the combined amount of unpaid losses and unearned premiums. More importantly, it has reinsurance recoverable of $319M and prepaid reinsurance premiums of $307M. Thanks to its continued ceding. If you look at its income statement, HRTG ceded 43.9% of its gross premiums earned. It decreased a bit YoY, but it’s because its policies in force decreased as well. This shows that HRTG keeps its conservative approach by spreading risks to its reinsurers, so even if stronger hurricanes land, it will have enough buffer to sustain its operations without having to borrow or bleed out cash.

Why I Won’t Rate It A Buy Yet

HRTG has a lot of risk mitigants. They may cushion hurricanes, competition, and macroeconomic volatility. However, they are not growth drivers to spur its expansion. After all, HRTG is still contracting its operations with its continued policy reductions. The consolation is that the quality of its operations has improved recently.

Another factor is valuation. I focused on its price ratios. With P/B, the stock is trading at 1.54x its book value, which is higher than the ideal ratio of 1.00x. This is also higher than the five-year average of 0.83x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP or target price will be $14.27. If I use P/E, the TP will be much higher at $25.92. But this still shows that HRTG is not cheap. I advise investors to stay on the sidelines.

Technicals

HRTG’s technicals have already waned in the past four months. You can see that the trend has already flattened with downside tendencies. Now, we can see some rebound attempts. But those appear inadequate to support further upside. The stock price is overlapping with the 50-day SMA, showing that the current momentum is already the same as the 50-day average. In addition, the 50-day SMA is trending downwards close to the 200-day SMA, which means that recent market sentiments have already weakened.

The same applies to its MACD and RSI. The MACD line is decreasing below the signal line amid the increasing selling volume. In addition, the histogram is already within negative territory, which implies that selling volume continues to outpace selling volume. Its RSI is also decreasing, showing decreasing momentum.

Conclusion

Heritage Insurance Holdings, Inc. remains fundamentally strong as it regains its growth and profitability with its strategic and selective underwriting policies. This is supported by its robust Balance Sheet and policy ceding to sustain its capacity while spreading risks. The only problem is that valuation is already high and shows limited upside potential. Technicals adhere to it as momentum wanes with increasing selling volume. I am giving this stock a hold rating.

data.title: JQC: This Fund Could Be A Very Useful Hedge If The Fed Does Not Cut (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund (JQC) is a closed-end fund that aims to provide its investors with a very high level of current income. The fund does actually manage to deliver reasonably well on this promise, as it boasts a 12.27% yield at the current share price. This is obviously much higher than the yields of just about any bond index fund, as well as most individual bonds. In fact, the current yield of a typical B-rated corporate bond issued by an American company today is about 7.41%, which is obviously well below the yield of JQC’s common shares. The fund has another advantage besides simply yield, and that is its diversification. As the fund’s portfolio consists of numerous individual debt securities, its actual exposure to any individual company is much lower than is the case for an investor who simply purchases a single high-yield debt security. This should result in investors in this fund having a lower risk of losing all of their investment to default, which is something that is always a risk when investing in high-yield debt. However, the specter of inflation is still very much present in today’s world, and as such, the real returns that investors in any type of debt security are likely to be much worse than their headline yields suggest. In addition, this fund could struggle to cover its distribution if the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates this year, but recent events make that less certain than a few months ago. As such, there are several things to consider before purchasing the shares of this fund.

The Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund Versus Debt Indices

As mentioned in the introduction, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund has a 12.27% yield at the current share price. This is substantially higher than the yield of any of the major American or global bond market indices. We can see that in this comparison chart:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.93%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.68%

Vanguard Total World Bond ETF (BNDW)

4.16%

Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF (BNDX)

4.45%

J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index (EMB)

5.07%

For quite some time now, the highest-yielding of the major American, global, or international bond indices is the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index, which tracks the price and yield performance of a group of junk bonds issued by American corporate entities. The J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index, which tracks the price and yield of a portfolio of U.S. dollar-denominated sovereign bonds issued by emerging market nations, has seen its yield increase fairly rapidly over the past few weeks, though. This is because the price of the index has fallen substantially since late February, when hostilities erupted between the United States, Israel, and Iran:

This chart shows the share price of the iShares J.P. Morgan USD Emerging Markets Bond ETF since the start of the year. The basic strategy of this exchange-traded index fund is to purchase all of the bonds that comprise the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index and then contract with a large financial company (the authorized participant) to create and destroy shares of the fund in order to keep the share price in line with the net asset value. Thus, the price of the exchange-traded index fund should be a pretty good proxy for the index itself. As we can see, the index really started declining in earnest on February 28, 2026, which was the same day that the United States and Israel began the first wave of airstrikes on Iran. That was the catalyst that prompted investors in most asset classes to adopt a risk-off stance and sell risky assets. Emerging market bonds (including ones issued by sovereign nations) are frequently considered to be riskier than sovereign bonds issued by developed nations, so they usually get sold off whenever investors become wary about risk. This was the case since the start of this month, and as such, investors can now obtain higher yields from emerging market bonds than was previously the case. However, the yield of the iShares J.P. Morgan USD Emerging Markets Bond ETF is still lower than the yield of American junk bond index funds as well as the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund.

However, the composition of all of the exchange-traded index funds that were just shown is somewhat different from the composition of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund. The fund’s most recent holdings report as of the time of writing is dated October 31, 2025. This document states that the fund had the following composition as of that date:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

% of Total Assets

Asset-Backed Securities

8.1%

4.9%

Common Stocks

0.9%

0.6%

Corporate Bonds

30.7%

18.7%

Variable Rate Senior Loan Interests

118.0%

71.8%

Warrants

0.0%

0.0%

Short-Term Investments

6.5%

4.0%

Perhaps the biggest difference between JQC’s portfolio and the portfolios of the exchange-traded index funds just mentioned is that the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund was primarily invested in variable-rate senior loans as of the end of October 2025. The index funds just shown, however, were entirely invested in fixed-rate traditional bonds. Those assets work very differently due to the simple fact that the coupon payments made by traditional bonds do not typically change over the lifetime of the bond. A variable-rate security, on the other hand, pays a coupon that pays an amount of money that changes whenever some interest-rate benchmark changes. For example, take a look at the stated yields of some of the holdings listed in JQC’s most recent schedule of investments:

We can see that nearly all of these securities explicitly state that their coupon will change based on the yield of either the Secured Overnight Financing Rate, the one-month Term SOFR, or the three-month Term SOFR rates. These are all short-term interest rate benchmarks used in the United States, and they are very heavily influenced by the short-term interest rate policy that is set by the Federal Reserve. The yield of traditional bonds, on the other hand, is not directly controlled by the central bank but, rather, is determined by private investors’ expectations with respect to inflation, default risk, and availability of alternative uses for the same money. In addition, and perhaps most importantly, the price of variable-rate debt securities does not typically change in response to interest rate movements. This is because the coupon rate of the security changes instead. As such, it is much more difficult to profit from the price movements of these securities than it is with traditional fixed-rate bonds. Rather, the only real way to make money from these securities is to simply collect the coupon payments that they pay out. This is important because this also means that traditional fixed-rate bonds are better securities to hold during periods of time in which interest rates are declining. Variable-rate securities are generally better securities to hold whenever interest rates are rising.

The fact that the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund had most of its assets invested in variable-rate securities in late October means that it is not necessarily comparable to the traditional bond index funds shown above. While it is true that both JQC and the bond index funds may attract consideration from investors who are looking to earn an income and invest in debt securities (which are usually considered to be less risky than common stocks), the market performance and income produced by the portfolios of each of the funds will differ. As such, it might be more appropriate to compare the yield of JQC to the yield of a variable-rate exchange-traded index fund. One fund that fits this criteria is the iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF (FLOT), which aims to track the price and yield performance of the Bloomberg US Floating Rate Note < 5 Years Index. This particular index fund has a 4.72% yield at the current share price, which is a bit better than some of the bond index funds, but still lower than the yield of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund.

However, even the iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF might not be perfectly comparable to JQC. This comes from the fact that the iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF is entirely invested in investment-grade debt securities. The Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund, on the other hand, is not. The fund’s website provides the following breakdown of its portfolio as of February 28, 2026:

An investment-grade debt security is anything that is rated BBB or higher. As we can see in the chart, that description only applies to 12.8% of JQC’s portfolio. The remainder of the fund’s assets is invested in speculative-grade securities, which are colloquially known as “junk bonds.” Speculative-grade securities almost always have higher yields than investment-grade securities, a feature that is intended to compensate investors for the higher default risk of these securities. As the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund is mostly invested in speculative-grade securities, we can expect its income to he higher than the income of FLOT, all else being equal. As both closed-end funds and ETFs distribute nearly all of their investment income to their shareholders, we would expect this to give the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund a higher yield. We do, in fact, see that.

There is, fortunately, an exchange-traded index fund that tracks the price and yield performance of an index of speculative-grade variable-rate debt securities. This is the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN), which yields 7.02% at the current share price. The Invesco Senior Loan ETF tracks the Morningstar LSTA US Leveraged Loan 100 Index, which is primarily an index of junk-rated variable-rate debt. The fact sheet for the index provides the following breakdown in terms of credit quality:

We can certainly see a difference between this and the portfolio of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund. In particular, JQC has a higher allocation to BB-rated debt than the index, and it also has a higher allocation to the lowest-quality debt ratings. This will have an impact on the relative yields of each of the funds, but we can immediately see that the Invesco Senior Loan ETF is more competitive with JQC in terms of yield than any of the other exchange-traded index funds. It is also the closest to JQC in terms of composition, so that makes sense.

The Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund Versus Peer Funds

As we just saw, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund has a considerably higher yield than any of the most comparable exchange-traded index funds. One reason for this appears to be that the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund is invested in somewhat riskier assets than many of the exchange-traded index funds. However, another reason is that JQC uses leverage in the management of its portfolio, whereas the index funds do not. The way that this works is that the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund borrows money and then uses that borrowed money to purchase debt securities. The securities that it purchases with this borrowed money make coupon payments to the fund, and the fund uses this income to pay the interest on its borrowings. If the coupon payments that the fund receives are higher than the interest on its own debt, then it is able to retain the surplus to boost the effective yield of the portfolio. This is usually going to be the case as the fund is buying junk debt, but should be able to borrow at investment-grade interest rates.

The most recent annual report for the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund corresponds to the full-year period that ended on July 31, 2025. For this full-year period, JQC received interest income of $107,289,805 from the debt securities that it had in its portfolio over the year. The fund’s average net assets over the same period were $819,170,296. Thus, the fund’s debt portfolio had an effective yield of 13.10% on net assets over the twelve-month period. This clearly shows that the fund has been able to boost its effective yield through the use of leverage. After all, the fund’s effective yield is in line with a portfolio that is entirely invested in CCC-rated bonds or lower:

The actual credit quality of the fund’s portfolio is better than this, though, a feat which it was able to achieve through the use of leverage.

The Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund is not the only fund that is able to employ leverage as a means of boosting the yield that it earns from its portfolio. In fact, most closed-end funds employ a similar strategy to achieve that same objective. As such, we can assume that other closed-end funds that employ similar strategies to the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund are likely to be somewhat more competitive in terms of yield. This is indeed the case, as shown in this chart:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund

12.27%

Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC)

12.29%

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

22.06%

abrdn Income Credit Strategies Fund (ACP)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Senior Loans

17.85%

We can immediately see that this group of thirteen closed-end funds has current yields that are all over the place. The median fund out of the peer group is 12.29%, which is slightly higher than the current yield of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund. However, it is possible that this is somewhat misleading. This is because it is questionable whether or not all of these funds will be able to sustain their current distributions. It is very important to investigate any fund that has a significantly higher yield than its peers very closely, as the high yield could be a sign that the market doubts the fund’s ability to sustain its distribution. One thing that can cause the market to react this way is the fund distributing more money than it actually earns from its investment portfolio and destroying its net asset value in the process. Ultimately, no fund can sustain such destruction of net asset value indefinitely since it will eventually run out of assets to distribute. This is the current situation for XFLT and ACP. This chart shows the net asset value per share of these two funds over the past three years:

As we can clearly see, the XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust has suffered a net asset value decline of 29.76%, and the abrdn Income Credit Strategies Fund has suffered a net asset value decline of 21.13% over the past three years. This is a clear sign that both funds are distributing more money than their portfolios actually earn and thus depleting their own asset base in the process. The same thing is true for the Carlyle Credit Income Fund, as its own website shows that the fund’s net asset value has fallen from $7.48 per share on January 31, 2025, to $3.69 per share on February 28, 2026. Thus, the very high distribution yields of all three of those funds are somewhat misleading, as they will likely have to cut in the near future, which will result in their investors ultimately receiving lower yields on their initial investment than they expected. In addition to this, the share prices of all three of those funds have deteriorated substantially over the past three years:

Thus, while CCIF, XFLT, and ACP may all appear to have very attractive yields on the surface, the actual returns that investors will end up receiving from them are less attractive. The income is high, but at the same time, your principal will be deteriorating. In fact, even if all of the distributions that each fund paid out were reinvested into additional shares of the fund on the distribution payment date, the only one of the three that delivered a positive total return over the past three years was ACP:

This is what I mean when I say that the distribution yields of some of those funds are misleading. It is not a case where the price of the fund will stay relatively stable while it pays out the distribution. Rather, those high-yielding funds are paying out more than they earn, and thus the assets backing each share of the fund are consistently declining.

Unfortunately, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund has the same problem, although its difficulties started much more recently. This chart shows the net asset value per share of JQC over the past three years:

As we can see, the net asset value of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund was relatively stable until early last year. It only really began to decline in earnest in late February 2025, but it stopped declining around mid-April 2025. The timing of this could suggest that the fund held some assets that got caught up in the widespread market sell-off that occurred in the first few months of 2025. However, the fund managed to partially recover from that sell-off over the spring and summer months, before once again entering into a period of declining net asset value that continues to this day.

If we look at the timing of these two net asset value declines along with the composition of the fund, it becomes fairly obvious what the cause of this was. The biggest cause was the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates during the final few months of both 2024 and 2025. As we saw earlier, a significant proportion of this fund’s assets is invested in variable-rate debt with coupon payments that are adjusted based on the Secured Overnight Financing Rate. This chart shows the Secured Overnight Financing Rate over the same three-year period as shown in the chart:

This chart compares the Secured Overnight Financing Rate to the Effective Federal Funds Rate:

We can see that there is a very high correlation between the two interest rates. In fact, the correlation between the two interest rate benchmarks is so high that we can pretty much state that the interest rate decisions of the Federal Reserve largely control the SOFR rate. This is very important for understanding the performance of JQC over the past few years. In short, the Federal Reserve cut its interest rate over the September 2024 to December 2024 period and over the September 2025 to December 2025 period. As it did this, the income received by the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund declined. However, the fund’s distribution remained stable from late 2023 through early 2026 (the fund cut it earlier this month after keeping it steady since late 2023). Thus, the fund’s income was declining, but JQC did not cut its distribution. As a result, it ended up overdistributing and depleting its net asset value. Fortunately, though, this fund did not end up destroying its net asset value to the same extent as CCIF, XFLT, and ACP. That is a positive sign for investors who are looking to ensure that their assets continue to support them for as long as possible, which likely describes most retirees and other traditional income investors.

Recent Performance

The last time that we discussed the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund was on December 31, 2025. At the time, I suggested selling the fund as the Federal Reserve was widely expected to reduce interest rates in 2026. Indeed, throughout most of 2025, President Trump was frequently demanding that the Federal Reserve aggressively cut interest rates “as soon as possible.” While the president of the United States does not have direct control over the Federal Reserve, he does have the power to appoint individuals to the Board of Governors, one of whom is also the chairman of the Federal Open Market Committee, which is responsible for setting the nation’s monetary policy and short-term interest rates. This power allows the president to influence the direction of the nation’s monetary policy, as the president could (and likely will) appoint individuals who are likely to have similar views on economics and the president’s economic agenda. Thus, President Trump is likely to appoint individuals who agree with his demand for near-term interest rate cuts. As of right now, President Trump has appointed Kevin Warsh to replace Jerome Powell when the latter’s term as Federal Reserve Chairman ends in May. While Warsh was critical of quantitative easing and the Fed’s ultra-low-interest rate policy following the financial crisis of the late 2000s, Mr. Warsh has also recently stated that he believes artificial intelligence will bring sufficient changes to the economy as to warrant near-term interest rate cuts. As we have already seen, if the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates, the income that JQC receives from its portfolio will decline, and this will ultimately force it to reduce its distribution.

However, there have been a few events that have occurred since my previous article on the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund that weaken the case for near-term interest rate cuts. One of the biggest of these changes is that inflation has started to move in the wrong direction. The Federal Reserve generally considers the Personal Consumption Expenditures Index to be a better measure of inflation than the more widely reported Consumer Price Index. According to Joseph G. Haubrich, economist emeritus for the Federal Reserve Bank of Cleveland:

The Federal Reserve, however, states its goal for inflation in terms of the PCE.

He goes on to state:

Over the short term, the core measure may give a more accurate reading of where inflation is headed.

Thus, the Federal Reserve may look most closely at the Core Personal Consumption Expenditures Index when it evaluates its success at controlling inflation. This chart shows the year-over-year change in the Core Personal Consumption Expenditures Index for every month in the past year:

The most recent Core Personal Consumption Expenditures Index figure shows a 3.10% rate of inflation. Furthermore, this index states that the rate of inflation was 2.8% in November and 3% in December 2025. Thus, inflation as measured by the Federal Reserve’s preferred gauge has been getting worse, and it is more than 50% above the so-called 2% target. This alone would weaken the case for near-term interest rate cuts.

However, there is another measurement that should not be ignored when talking about inflation. This is the Producer Price Index, which measures the average change over time in the selling prices received by domestic producers of products. This chart shows the month-over-month rate of change in the Producer Price Index for each of the past twelve months:

While headlines and even economists frequently focus on the year-over-year inflation measurement figures rather than the month-over-month figures, the month-over-month figures are still very important because they provide a much more real-time picture of how prices are changing. With this in mind, look at the trend since October. The month-over-month change in the Producer Price Index was 0.1% in October 2025, and it has since increased to 0.7% in February. That is an annualized rate of change of 8.4%, obviously far above the Federal Reserve’s so-called 2% inflation target. It is unlikely that commercial producers and businesses will eat cost increases of this level indefinitely, so at some point, this will make its way down into consumer prices. As such, this trend weakens the case for near-term interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve, as it points to a worsening inflation outlook that the central bank will likely want to contain.

It is important to note that the just-described worsening inflation outlook was before another event occurred that will likely make inflation much worse in the near term. This was, of course, the outbreak of hostilities between the United States, Israel, and Iran that resulted in passage through the Strait of Hormuz becoming disrupted and a rapid increase in the price of crude oil. Since the start of March 2026, Brent crude oil has risen by 38.77%, and West Texas Intermediate crude oil has risen by 32.57%. Crude oil is an input into most products due to the simple fact that nearly everything that is physical needs to be transported in some way. In addition, many machines run on refined crude oil, and even most service employees have to pay for gasoline to travel from place to place. As such, we have a situation in which inflation was getting worse, and then crude oil prices spiked. This strongly suggests that inflation will continue to get worse before it gets better. This could make it difficult for the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates for a while.

The market seems to have caught on to this, and recent trends suggest that it agrees with this thesis. Right now, the market strongly believes that there will be either no rate cuts at all or a rate hike in 2026:

As of right now, the Federal Reserve’s current federal funds target rate is 350 to 375 basis points. Thus, the market currently thinks that there is a 54.2% chance that the Federal Reserve keeps its benchmark interest rate unchanged for the remainder of this year. There is only a 1.3% chance of a single 25-basis point cut. There is currently nobody expecting more than a single interest rate cut. The market also believes that there is a 44.7% chance of between one and four 25-basis point interest rate hikes this year.

If the market is correct, this completely changes the thesis for variable-rate securities since the last time that we discussed the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund. If the Federal Reserve were to increase interest rates, then it would increase the amount of money that this fund receives from the variable-rate assets in its portfolio. That could potentially point to a distribution increase in the future once the rate hikes increase the fund’s income sufficiently. This also suggests that variable-rate securities will outperform traditional fixed-rate bonds for the near term. Thus, if the market is correct here, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund should be upgraded from a “Sell” to a “Buy.”

Despite this, the fund’s performance since the last time that we discussed it has been rather disappointing. As we can see here, the fund’s share price has declined 3.59% since our last discussion. This is a bit worse than the price performance of several different debt indices:

While this is disappointing, it is also not unexpected. As we discussed earlier, the Invesco Senior Loan ETF is perhaps the closest of the group to JQC in terms of composition. That particular fund has declined by 2.38% since the last time that we looked at JQC. The Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund employs leverage, whereas BKLN does not. One of the disadvantages of leverage is that it amplifies downward movements in asset prices. Thus, we would expect this fund’s price to decline more than BKLN during any period in which asset prices are falling.

Fortunately, though, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund did manage to do somewhat better over the period in terms of total return:

As we can see, if we assume reinvestment of all of the distributions paid out by all of the funds, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund provided its investors with a 0.49% total loss. This is still a loss, but it is still much better than many other things performed during the same period. After all, the S&P 500 Index (SP500) has declined by 3.70% over the same period. Investors who reinvested their distributions into additional shares of JQC have at least managed to come fairly close to keeping the value of their investment stable in the face of the market carnage.

Valuation

Shares of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund are currently trading at a 12.27% discount on net asset value. This is a much more attractive discount than the 9.61% discount that the fund has had on average over the past month.

The current price of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund is also very attractive compared to the fund’s historical pricing. Over the past five years, shares of JQC have traded at prices ranging from a 0.34% discount to a 15.85% discount on net asset value. The average price over the five-year period has been an 8.25% discount on net asset value. Obviously, the current price represents a larger discount than the fund’s five-year average, and thus it looks reasonably attractive as an entry point.

Here is how the current price of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund compares with that of its peers:

Fund Name

Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund

As with their yields, the valuations of these various funds are all over the place. The median price of the thirteen-fund group is a 12.68% discount, which is obviously very slightly better than the current price of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund. However, some of these funds are not really worth purchasing at all due to the high level of net asset value destruction. Most of the funds that are overdistributing are the ones that have very high discounts to net asset value, and if we exclude those from consideration, then the current price of the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund looks reasonably attractive to the rest of its peer group. When this is combined with the fund’s large discount compared to its own historical valuation, the current price looks quite reasonable for any investor looking to buy shares of it as a hedge against rising interest rates.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund is a closed-end fund that has the bulk of its assets in variable-rate corporate debt. While that was not an especially attractive composition a few months ago, the thesis for holding variable-rate debt has improved significantly. This is because both inflation data and the recent oil price shock have weakened the case for near-term interest rate cuts and, in fact, may have made a near-term interest rate hike a realistic possibility. Variable-rate securities are among the few asset classes that benefit from rising interest rates, and as such, this fund could help offset losses in assets such as stocks or bonds that typically decline whenever interest rates rise. As such, it is appropriate to upgrade this fund to a “buy” for now. The Federal Reserve still seems committed to cutting interest rates this year (based on the most recent dot plot), so I would not recommend taking a huge position in this fund, but it could be helpful to own it as a way to ride through the current environment and earn some income along the way.

data.title: Coal's Comeback And Four Stocks To Focus On
data.description:
Introduction

Once again, and this time more severely, global oil and gas flows have been disrupted, and coal may come back to save the day. With around 17% of global LNG production trapped until the war is over and 3% offline for a few years due to infrastructure damage, Japanese, Korean, and European electricity plants will need to switch to coal to reduce costs or, in some cases, avoid blackouts. Thermal coal prices have risen from depressed levels and should provide the sector with higher margins and earnings for a few years, perhaps longer, as the world attempts to reduce conflict-zone risk in its energy supplies and ultimately moves toward clean nuclear energy and more renewables.

Coal Price Parity

As LNG supplies decrease and prices rise, power plants that use it to generate electricity may first switch to coal to reduce costs, which requires regulatory approval. Under this scenario, coal should trade at a discount to LNG parity. Today, with LNG prices around $20/MTU, high-grade thermal coal’s parity is around $152 on a caloric value basis. Many US coal companies have lower quality grades and compete with cheap natural gas. Australia generally has far higher grades and is closer to the Japanese/Korean markets, with lower transport costs. Europe may also switch to coal, but Poland has sufficient supplies that could meet demand. Nonetheless, if LNG is in deficit, then coal is the most likely to replace it in electricity generation, and the parity price may be reached.

Energy Price Spikes

The Iran war and subsequent infrastructure damage and transport closure (Hormuz) drove oil and LNG prices up by over 80%, but coal is still up around 20%. The Ukrainian invasion led to a far greater spike in LNG and coal prices. The ratio of Coal to LNG, which is normally below 10x, reached 25x during the Ukrainian impact and is now approaching 15x. There seems to be room for higher LNG and coal prices in the long term, as this conflict lasts longer, with consequences felt for many years. The market may add conflict premiums to Gulf LNG, which can push coal prices higher until new fuel and energy sources replace it.

Coal Company Comps

I gathered consensus data from a wide range of coal miners worldwide. I assume most forecasts do not yet reflect higher coal prices, but they are starting to trickle in. The sector has been largely forgotten, given the move to clean energy, and as demand fell, so have earnings for most companies. The market has, however, begun to price in better results, with price targets in negative territory driven by the Iran war. While some companies seem to have exceptional growth forecasts for 2026, most are due to a difficult 2025, when oversupply in Indonesia drove many companies into losses. Thus, the growth rates are from depressed levels. Core Natural Resources (CNR) has high growth due to M&A and recovery. Valuations are low, as expected for a mature and declining sector, with most stocks trading under 6x EV/EBITDA. On the positive side, the sector has a solid balance sheet with net cash positions.

Sensitivity Analysis

To better gauge the upside potential of higher coal prices for the sector, I conducted a sensitivity analysis for Yancoal Australia (YACAF), Whitehaven Coal (WHITF), and two US companies, CNR and Peabody (BTU). Using the current consensus forecast, I created three price scenarios. Since each company has different coal grades and geographic variations, I assumed a price increase relative to their current forecasts. In my view, 25% is a conservative estimate for the next year or two. A 50% increase may require further delays in ending the conflict, and a 75% increase would put LNG production in deficit for two years or more.

I maintained current volumes and cost structure, so the high prices go to margins and EBITDA. I then valued the four stocks at 3x EV/EBITDA without adjusting for higher cash flow. I think this is a conservative valuation approach. The results, as shown in the tables below, could drive shares higher by more than 50% in the most conservative case. A long war and/or sustained LNG disruption could lead to shares more than doubling from here.

Risk

There are two key risks to coal price increases: that the war ends soon and that LNG supplies normalize quickly. A secondary risk is regulation, the ability to switch power plants to polluting coal. Finally, there is a transport risk: can the interested parties secure bulk ships at reasonable rates to meet delivery demand?

Conclusion

Given LNG capacity curtailment and destruction, it is highly likely that many nations dependent on this fuel to produce electricity will be forced to substitute it for coal, at first due to price pressures on inflation, and then due to shortages that may provoke blackouts. Coal is the only readily available and economically viable substitute for LNG. Prices should continue to rise, and the coal companies can generate windfall profits that will drive share valuation and increase capital returns.
