data.title: Celestica: This Dip Is A Massive Opportunity
data.description:
CLS Stock Still Looks Promising On Its Dip

My previous article on Celestica Inc. (CLS) was basically a preview research that helped me come to the conclusion that the firm was about to beat the consensus once again, which is usually a bullish catalyst. That's why I kept my rating unchanged back then.

Historically, management's midpoints have been placed conservatively, and the strength of end markets, combined with solid operating leverage, has allowed CLS to exceed even the high ends. Therefore, it is more appropriate to compare the current consensus with Celestica management's most bullish Q4 targets - $3.575 billion in sales and $1.81 in EPS. Assuming a slight 2% beat to these high ends, Celestica could deliver a double beat of 4.48% in sales and about 4.89% in adjusted EPS.

From my preview article on Celestica's results.

The Q4 results came out in late January, and Celestica double-beat the consensus indeed, beating both top and bottom lines by 4.77% and 7.27%, respectively.

I was pretty close to the top line beat expectations, but clearly underestimated the margins' sustainability, and so the EPS beat was way stronger than I anticipated initially.

Anyway, the stock's price action hasn't improved even after this catalyst appeared—CLS is down by over 9% since my preview article was published, and it's down 7.52% on a YTD basis:

Despite these bearish moves and the price consolidation that just can't stop, I believe CLS still looks good for long-term buyers because the tailwinds I foresaw in my previous takes are valid to date, and in some cases, they've become even more obvious. Plus, the valuation reset looks healthy amid no forward earnings growth rate cuts from the market—investors are basically getting the same high-quality growth stock at a less expensive price, which is great to see. From all this, I think CLS should be rated as a “Buy”—the current consolidation is unlikely to hold for too long if Celestica executes the way it did throughout 2020-2025.

My Updated Reasoning On CLS

In Q4, CLS reported $3.655 billion in sales (+44% YoY), surpassing the high end of the management's own guidance, and the adjusted EPS came in at $1.89, which was 70% above last year's level. The operating leverage stayed in place despite the consensus expecting normalization and some sort of a cool-off on this front. It's clear to me that the revenue mix shift toward the CCS segment (“Connectivity and Cloud Solutions”) and Celestica's dominant market share in its niche create strong support for the continued margin expansion. Yes, the key margins won't grow the way they did in 2023-2025 if we forecast the next few years, but the Q4 results have shown that the positive discrepancy between the forward earnings growth rates and the forward revenue growth rates should stay with us for longer than the market had been pricing in.

The HPS business (“Hardware Platform Solutions”) generated $1.4 billion in sales in Q4, and we saw a YoY growth of 72%, with the revenue share now accounting for ~38% of the whole pie. HPS is bringing a higher margin because they handle customized end-to-end platform designs—that's why we've seen a 90-basis point improvement in adjusted income margins on a consolidated basis. From the updated guidance, we see that the CCS segment is expected to see FY2026 revenue YoY growth of 50%, and the servers & storage revenues (“Enterprise” sales below) should increase “one hundred-high teens” in Q1 2026, per the latest IR presentation.

I don't see any reason why the Enterprise revenue growth will decelerate starting in Q2 2026. The management mentioned in the presentations that they're going to be “ramping AI/ML compute volumes,” and it's about to “accelerate into 2026 with strong demand continuing into 2027.” Hyperscalers' demand is set to remain strong, as shown below.

So, if the Enterprise and Communications revenue growth rates don't decelerate in Q2-Q4 2026, I believe we shall see at least 1-2 bullish updates to the already-bullish FY2026 guidance that the management shared in Q4:

To achieve stable business growth in selected niches, CLS will spend a lot on CAPEX ($1 billion throughout 2026), but it's going to be only 6% of the updated revenue target for the year. I don't think it's too risky, especially if they update the guidance once or twice in 2026. And what's important—they're funding the expenditures with the internally generated cash flows, planning no external capital raises, as far as I understand. I think the management seems to have great visibility into the long-term earnings generation.

The prevailing negative that seemingly prevented CLS's price action from breaking out of today's consolidation phase was the Digitimes report suggesting that Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) might expand assembly orders for its L10-L11 TPU servers to other Taiwanese manufacturers like Inventec, thus challenging the long-term moat CLS used to have. But the updated guidance that only showed improvements and the analysts' revisions that followed the Q4 updates seem to contradict the above news piece. Even on margins, the analysts have upgraded their previous estimations—Google's potential decision doesn't seem to be hurting the fundamentals of CLS anyhow (at least for now).

Plus, we've recently seen another important catalyst that hasn't yet led to CLS's repricing higher, although I think it should have. I'm talking about the recently announced strategic partnership with Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD):

The companies said that at launch, Celestica will undertake the research and development, or R&D, design, and manufacturing of scale-up networking switches in the AMD Helios rack-scale AI architecture.

AMD Helios is a high-performance, open-standard AI rack-scale infrastructure designed for large-scale AI training and inference. Rack-scale AI architecture is a computing approach where an entire rack, rather than an individual server, acts as the foundational unit for AI workloads. It integrates computing power like graphics processing units, or GPUs, high-speed networking, and liquid cooling into a single system to efficiently train large language models, or LLMs.

The companies said scale-up switches will use advanced networking silicon to enable the high-speed interconnect of the next-generation AMD Instinct MI450 Series GPUs, enabling leading-edge computing optimized for large-scale AI clusters. AMD Helios will be available to customers in late 2026.

Source: Seeking Alpha News [March 2026].

As CLS is going to contribute to the key stages of the high-performance scale-up networking switches within the Helios architecture, it will move up the AI infrastructure value chain (from ODM to a system-level rack architect), which is likely to lead to higher margins in the next few years.

The core business trend that should last for the next few years and drive up CLS's financials is, of course, the AI clusters scaling out to hundreds of thousands of chips, which is forcing hyperscalers to aggressively upgrade from 400G switches to 800G and then to 1.6T environments. Celestica is already feeling the impact as it exited 2025 with an estimated 50% split between 800G and 400G switch deployments. Assuming that they ramp the 1.6T networking switch platform into mass production in 2027, they're likely to see their current (and planned) CAPEX translate into revenues quite quickly. That's why analysts see a 3-year CAGR of sales of ~34%:

Assuming that CLS trades at 2x sales in FY2027, and it beats the current sales consensus by the historical average of the past 3 years (~0.76%), the resulting market cap should be at ~$49 billion:

(0.0076+1) * $24.3 * 2x = ~$49 billion.

Source: Oakoff's calculations, SA data.

It looks like a conservative estimate because the exit P/S multiple might actually be higher (at 3-4x, given the revenue ramp-up rates). So, I see an upside potential of at least 55.9% to the current market cap.

Risks

As I pointed out in my previous article on CLS, I might be overlooking the possible negatives of customer concentration. In Q4, we saw that the company's top 10 customers represented ~80% of its consolidated sales, up from 73% in 2024—it's a huge jump on a YoY basis, to be fair. I believe Celestica's clients depend on its products, and, therefore, the “Big 3” will not be leaving anytime soon. However, some of them might decide to produce certain components in-house, which could reduce the need for CLS's products to some extent. If that happens, CLS's top line will be hit hard, and my bullish upgrade will age poorly over the medium-term horizon.

Moreover, my valuation calculations may be off because they're based on a single metric. In reality, the stock's P/E and EV/EBITDA ratios look stretched because the business naturally has lower margins compared to the median margins within the IT sector.

If CLS drops to 20x FY2027 earnings and we see 0 premium on top of today's FY2027 EPS consensus, the resulting price target would be close to $257.8/share—that's a downside potential of 5-6%.

Your Takeaway

The risks may still be there, and CLS might contract by another 10-20% from today's levels, but I think it won't be driven by the forward-looking fundamentals. In other words, I see limited negatives to Celestica's business in the next 2-3 years, as it's expected to grow its sales at a 33% CAGR and ultimately improve margins (vs. 2025 levels). For the level of growth investors are getting from CLS, the stock looks cheap to me, although some multiples exceed the median of the general IT sector. For me, CLS remains a solid buy at today's price levels.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: WFC-D Vs. WFC-Y: Comparing Coupon Extremes
data.description:
Introduction

Fans of the Music Man will understand the photo choice as their early history was built around their wagon service, delivering mail and household goods out west. From those roots, Wells Fargo & Company (WFC) is now one of the largest US-based banks.

As Wells Fargo has six preferred stocks to pick from, I chose the ones with the lowest or highest coupon amongst the $25 Par issues, those being:

  • Wells Fargo & Co 4.25% Dep Shares Non-Cumul Perp Cls A Preferred Stock Series DD (WFC.PR.D)
  • Wells Fargo & Co., 5.625% Dep Shares Non-Cumul Class A Preferred Stock Series Y (WFC.PR.Y)

If picking between these two, my Buy rating would be for the Series D. A +6% yield is decent for the low risk in ownership.

Understanding Wells Fargo & Company

Seeking Alpha describes this bank as (edited):

Wells Fargo & Company, a financial services company, provides diversified banking, investment, mortgage, and consumer and commercial finance products and services in the United States and internationally. It operates through four segments: Consumer Banking and Lending; Commercial Banking; Corporate and Investment Banking; and Wealth and Investment Management. The company’s financial products and services include checking and savings accounts, and credit and debit cards, as well as home, auto, personal, and small business lending services. It also provides personalized wealth management, brokerage, financial planning, lending, private banking, trust and fiduciary products and services; and financial solutions to private, family owned and public companies through products and services including banking and credit products across multiple industry sectors and municipalities. In addition, it offers a suite of capital markets, banking, and financial products and services, such as corporate banking, investment banking, treasury management, commercial real estate lending and servicing, equity, and fixed income solutions. Wells Fargo & Company was founded in 1852 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

Source: seekingalpha WFC

By assets, Wells Fargo is the third largest U.S. bank behind JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Bank of America (BAC). The bank divides their operations into the follow segments:

  • Consumer banking and lending
  • Commercial banking
  • Corporate and Investment Banking
  • Wealth and Investment Management

This map shows the branch layout for Wells Fargo: they like the populated coasts.

The next table shows how it performed across these segments in 2025.

Bank investors should be interested in how well the bank is capitalized. The 2025 Annual Report provides these diagrams showing Wells Fargo's levels. The report states that 8.5% is the required minimum.

As is my practice, let us see how well the $1B in annual preferred dividends are covered by Net Income.

Recent years shows coverage at 17X to 20X, one of the best I have come across. Next is to see the Total Common Equity/PFD Par ratio. The bank has $17.3B in outstanding preferreds based on liquidation value.

This provides a 9.5X coverage, strong but not the highest I have seen from major banks. That said, missing payments or defaulting on the preferreds seems extremely remote.

Preferred stock analysis

The next table compares key features.

Factor Series DD Series Y
Issued 7/20/21 4/17/17
Size (MM) 50 24
Coupon 4.25% 5.625%
Price $17.13 $23.70
Yield 6.2% 5.9%
Call date 7/20/26 6/15/22
YTC 142% NA

Series D was rated BBB when issued but that was lowered two notches to BB+ last year. Dividends are non-cumulative but qualify for the preferential income tax rate.

  • Series D provides a limited amount of Call protection. Despite it becoming Callable soon, it being the lowest coupon, that gives it the best Call protection amongst all currently Callable issues.
  • Based on its current price, investors are not expecting Series D to be called.
  • Between these two, the Series D offers the higher yield.
  • There are several other preferreds with similar yield but with higher coupons that logically have more Call risk.
  • Yields are comparable to what other large US bank preferreds offer.
  • If picking between these two, my Buy rating would be for the Series D. A +6% yield is decent for the low risk in ownership.

Portfolio strategy

For those that want total control of what preferred stocks they hold, thus not using funds, Seeking Alpha articles are a good first step. Screen sites such as QuantumOnline.com is another I use to find similar issues based on issuer, coupon, rating, and Call dates.

Evaluating preferred stocks boils down to a few key questions to ask:

  • Do I believe in the issuer enough to not fear missing a dividend payout, especially if non-cumulative, or its redemption value?
  • If currently or soon-to-be callable, will it? If they think so, then how does the YTC measure up compared to other preferred shares available?
  • If the coupon floats, where do I see rates going if I am not a long-term holder where it is of less concern?

Final Note

Wells Fargo, as of the end of 2025, had eight preferred stocks, summarized in this chart, with more details provide by this link.

An investor considering holding Wells Fargo preferreds should take the time to investigate all the options, not just the two reviewed by me.
